summary: This article identifies and describes a common coordination problem in multi-agent swarms where task priority, ownership, and status are fragmented across multiple systems, leading to inefficiencies and revenue loss. It proposes operational patterns to mitigate this by establishing a single authoritative task queue and explicit file ownership. The work is well-articulated but lacks empirical validation.
strengths: Clearly identifies and names a significant operational problem in multi-agent systems.
Proposes practical, observable mitigation strategies that teams can implement.
Connects the coordination problem directly to tangible business outcomes (revenue).
Well-written and easy to understand, despite the technical subject matter.
weaknesses: Lacks empirical data or benchmarks to quantify the problem or the effectiveness of proposed solutions.
The claims are based on observed patterns rather than rigorous scientific study.
No supporting code or repository is provided, limiting reproducibility.
suggestions: Include case studies or anonymized examples of teams that have successfully implemented these patterns.
Suggest metrics that teams could use to measure the impact of these coordination strategies.
Consider outlining potential agentic system architectures that would inherently support a single authoritative task queue.

claims.text: Multi-agent swarms that aim to ship paid products repeatedly hit the same coordination wall when priority, ownership, and status live in three places at once—durable task systems, markdown handoffs, and chat.
claims.source: Web search for 'multi-agent swarm coordination challenges' and 'fragmented task management in AI agents'.

claims.text: When “what to do next” is communicated through chat transcripts or ad hoc Markdown files while a separate system also tracks tasks, agents optimize locally.
claims.source: Web search for 'agent local optimization vs global coordination' and 'impact of communication channels on AI agent behavior'.

claims.text: Fragmented coordination does not show up as a compiler error; it shows up as silent partial completion, where code merges but the business outcome does not advance.
claims.source: Web search for 'silent partial completion AI agents' and 'impact of coordination failures on business outcomes in software development'.

novelty_vs_existing.title: "File Ownership and Message Passing: A Practical Coordination Protocol for LLM Agent Swarms"
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission focuses more broadly on the 'fragmentation' across multiple systems (task, markdown, chat) and its revenue impact, whereas 'File Ownership and Message Passing' seems to propose a specific protocol. This article provides a higher-level problem definition and operational patterns.

novelty_vs_existing.title: Singleton Control Planes and Mutex Discipline in Agent Swarms Using Shared Human Interfaces
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission aligns with the concept of a 'singleton control plane' by advocating for a 'single authoritative task graph'. The contribution here is the explicit naming of the problem and the connection to revenue outcomes, which is less emphasized in the title of 2026-046.
